attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department of a tertiary referral centre with a three-day history of worsening lower back pain. She underwent a caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia 10 days ago. She has a history of gestational diabetes which was diet-controlled. On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37°C. She has marked tenderness over her lower lumbar spine. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation in both legs and weakness in her left foot dorsiflexion. Blood tests show a white cell count of 14 x 10^9/L (4-11) and C-reactive protein of 110 mg/L (<5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: CT scan of the lumbar spine
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs suggestive of an epidural abscess, a rare but serious complication of neuraxial anaesthesia. The symptoms of back pain, tenderness and neurological deficits along with her recent history of spinal anaesthesia, strongly point towards this diagnosis. In this scenario, the most appropriate next step is to perform an MRI of the spine.

MRI with contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing epidural abscess. It provides excellent visualization of the spinal cord, epidural space, and surrounding soft tissues, allowing for accurate localization and characterization of the abscess. This information is crucial for planning further management, including potential surgical intervention.

A CT scan of the lumbar spine is less sensitive than MRI for detecting epidural abscesses, especially in the early stages. While it may show some abnormalities, it is not the preferred imaging modality for this condition.

Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in suspected epidural abscess due to the risk of introducing infection into the subarachnoid space or causing herniation if there is significant mass effect. Moreover, it does not provide the necessary anatomical information for management.

Ultrasound guided drainage is not appropriate at this stage. While ultrasound-guided drainage can be used for some superficial abscesses, it is not suitable for epidural abscesses. These abscesses are deep-seated and require precise localization and careful surgical approach to avoid damaging the spinal cord or nerves. Attempting drainage without proper imaging and neurosurgical expertise could lead to severe complications.

Urgent neurosurgical referral will likely be necessary, but it should follow imaging confirmation of the diagnosis. The neurosurgeon will need the MRI results to plan any potential surgical intervention.

After confirming the diagnosis with MRI, management typically involves a combination of surgical decompression and targeted antibiotic therapy. The choice between conservative management with antibiotics alone or surgical intervention depends on the extent of the abscess and the severity of neurological deficits. Prompt treatment is crucial to prevent permanent neurological damage.
learningPoints: Epidural abscess is a rare but serious complication of neuraxial anaesthesia, presenting with symptoms such as back pain, fever, tenderness and neurological deficits.
MRI with contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing epidural abscess.
Prompt surgical decompression and targeted antibiotic therapy are crucial in managing epidural abscess to prevent permanent neurological damage.
references: Tetsuka S, Suzuki T, Ogawa T, Hashimoto R, Kato H. Spinal Epidural Abscess: A Review Highlighting Early Diagnosis and Management. JMA J. 2020 Jan 15;3(1):29-40. doi: 10.31662/jmaj.2019-0038. Epub 2019 Oct 24. PMID: 33324773; PMCID: PMC7733760. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33324773/

Sharfman ZT, Gelfand Y, Shah P, Holtzman AJ, Mendelis JR, Kinon MD, Krystal JD, Brook A, Yassari R, Kramer DC. Spinal Epidural Abscess: A Review of Presentation, Management, and Medicolegal Implications. Asian Spine J. 2020 Oct;14(5):742-759. doi: 10.31616/asj.2019.0369. Epub 2020 Jul 29. PMID: 32718133; PMCID: PMC7595828.
